artifacts.sourceTitle: Assessment of Courses Procedures
artifacts.queryUsed: site:auckland.ac.nz artificial intelligence assessment procedures two-lane
artifacts.verificationNotes: Official University of Auckland Policy Hub procedure page. The assessment design procedure directly states the two-lane AI assessment rule.

artifacts.sourceTitle: Advice for students on using Artificial Intelligence
artifacts.queryUsed: site:auckland.ac.nz University of Auckland generative AI assessment policy students
artifacts.verificationNotes: Official student guidance page under auckland.ac.nz. Content covers AI in assessment, authorship responsibility, and Lane 1/Lane 2 use.

artifacts.sourceTitle: Guidelines on permitted use of software in assessment activities
artifacts.queryUsed: site:auckland.ac.nz generative AI academic integrity permitted use software assessment
artifacts.verificationNotes: Official academic integrity guideline page. Content identifies Gen-AI as software that may not be permitted in some assessment activities and links non-permitted software use to academic integrity.

artifacts.sourceTitle: Two-Lane Approach to assessment
artifacts.verificationNotes: Official University of Auckland TeachWell guidance page. The page explains the two-lane assessment model, implementation timing, and AI detection stance.

artifacts.sourceTitle: Learn about the University's AI Usage Standard
artifacts.queryUsed: site:teachwell.auckland.ac.nz University AI Usage Standard data classification
artifacts.verificationNotes: Official TeachWell public explainer for the University's Generative Artificial Intelligence Usage Standard. The underlying standard itself is login-gated, so claims are scoped to the public explainer text.

artifacts.sourceTitle: Student guidelines for using Artificial Intelligence (AI) in taught courses

artifacts.verificationNotes: Official Auckland Law School student guidance page. Content is faculty-specific and complements University policy, so claims are scoped to Law taught courses.

artifacts.sourceTitle: The use of AI tools in coursework
artifacts.queryUsed: site:teachwell.auckland.ac.nz AI tools in coursework University of Auckland
artifacts.verificationNotes: Official TeachWell guidance page for educators. Content gives teaching guidance rather than a binding policy rule, so no separate claim candidate was produced from this source.

artifacts.verificationNotes: The source is an official intranet standard linked from a public page, but public fetch through Firecrawl failed with document_antibot and the URL path indicates intranet access. No bypass was attempted.

artifacts.evidenceSnippet: This article reflects the opinion of the authors and not necessarily the views of Waipapa Taumata Rau University of Auckland.
artifacts.notes: Rejected as source evidence for policy claims because it is a news/opinion analysis article rather than a policy or guidance page.

artifacts.evidenceSnippet: Public explainer links to the standard with a login required marker; Firecrawl fetch failed with document_antibot.
artifacts.notes: Marked inaccessible/blocked. The crawler did not bypass intranet access controls, hard WAF, or antibot restrictions.

methodsAttempted.query: site:auckland.ac.nz University of Auckland generative AI assessment policy students
methodsAttempted.notes: Firecrawl search found official student AI advice, Law faculty guidance, permitted-software assessment guidelines, and TeachWell assessment guidance.

methodsAttempted.query: site:auckland.ac.nz Artificial intelligence University of Auckland assessment
methodsAttempted.notes: Search identified both policy/guidance pages and a news/opinion article that was rejected for low policy specificity.

methodsAttempted.query: site:auckland.ac.nz assessment courses procedures artificial intelligence two-lane
methodsAttempted.notes: Official Policy Hub Assessment of Courses Procedures page was selected as the highest-authority source for the two-lane AI assessment rule.
Public TeachWell AI Usage Standard explainer linked to an intranet standard; the intranet standard was marked inaccessible without bypass.

artifacts.summary: The crawl discovered multiple official University of Auckland public sources for AI assessment, academic integrity, and data/privacy guidance. The strongest binding source is the Policy Hub Assessment of Courses Procedures. Two candidates were rejected or blocked: a news/opinion page and a login-gated intranet standard.

stopConditions: Stop after public official sources have enough direct evidence for scoped claim candidates.

Reject news-only, event-only, research showcase, and low-policy-specificity pages.

artifacts.claimText: The University of Auckland's Assessment of Courses Procedures state that AI use in assessment tasks may only be restricted when the task is a controlled assessment, identified as Lane 1; AI may be used without restriction in other assessment tasks, identified as Lane 2.
artifacts.normalizedValue: Two-lane assessment rule: AI restrictions only for controlled Lane 1 tasks; Lane 2 unrestricted

citation.sourceRights: Tracker metadata is open licensed. Official source documents, page text, PDFs, and other source materials retain their original rights and terms.

artifacts.sourceTitle: Assessment of Courses Procedures
artifacts.evidenceSnippetOriginal: In accordance with the University's two-lane approach, the use of artificial intelligence (AI) in assessment tasks may only be restricted if the assessment task is a controlled assessment (lane 1). AI may be used without restriction in all other assessment tasks (lane 2).
artifacts.evidenceSnippetDisplay: The assessment procedure limits AI restrictions to controlled Lane 1 assessments and says AI may be used without restriction in Lane 2 tasks.
artifacts.rightsNote: Tracker metadata is open licensed. Official source documents, page text, PDFs, and other source materials retain their original rights and terms.

citation.sourceRights: Tracker metadata is open licensed. Official source documents, page text, PDFs, and other source materials retain their original rights and terms.

artifacts.notes: Approved as a narrow claim directly supported by the official Assessment of Courses Procedures text. No canonical publication flag is set.

artifacts.claimText: The University of Auckland's Assessment of Courses Procedures require courses to use the two-lane nomenclature, including telling students which assessments align with Lane 1 or Lane 2, and require courses and programmes to implement the two-lane approach in assessment design by 2027.
artifacts.normalizedValue: Courses must label assessment lanes and implement two-lane design by 2027

citation.sourceRights: Tracker metadata is open licensed. Official source documents, page text, PDFs, and other source materials retain their original rights and terms.

artifacts.sourceTitle: Assessment of Courses Procedures
artifacts.evidenceSnippetOriginal: Courses must use nomenclature of the two-lane approach, including specifying to students which assessments align with lane 1 or lane 2. Courses and programmes will be required to implement the two-lane approach in assessment design by 2027.
artifacts.evidenceSnippetDisplay: Courses must identify Lane 1/Lane 2 assessments and implement the two-lane assessment approach by 2027.
artifacts.rightsNote: Tracker metadata is open licensed. Official source documents, page text, PDFs, and other source materials retain their original rights and terms.

citation.sourceRights: Tracker metadata is open licensed. Official source documents, page text, PDFs, and other source materials retain their original rights and terms.

artifacts.notes: Approved because the claim closely follows the official procedure wording on lane nomenclature and 2027 implementation.

artifacts.claimText: The University of Auckland's student AI advice states that AI has no agency, treats the student prompting an AI tool as the author, and says students are ultimately responsible for work submitted for assessment.
artifacts.normalizedValue: Student remains author/responsible party for AI-generated assessment work

citation.citationTitle: Advice for students on using Artificial Intelligence
citation.sourceRights: Tracker metadata is open licensed. Official source documents, page text, PDFs, and other source materials retain their original rights and terms.

artifacts.sourceTitle: Advice for students on using Artificial Intelligence
artifacts.evidenceSnippetOriginal: The university takes the position that AI has no agency. What this means is that the student who is prompting the AI tool is to be treated as the author. As the author, each student is responsible for the work generated by the AI tool, and this may include expressly acknowledging the use of the tool.
artifacts.evidenceSnippetDisplay: The student AI advice treats the student prompting AI as the author and responsible for AI-generated work.
artifacts.rightsNote: Tracker metadata is open licensed. Official source documents, page text, PDFs, and other source materials retain their original rights and terms.

citation.citationTitle: Advice for students on using Artificial Intelligence
citation.sourceRights: Tracker metadata is open licensed. Official source documents, page text, PDFs, and other source materials retain their original rights and terms.

artifacts.notes: Approved as a direct student-facing academic-integrity claim supported by official student guidance.

artifacts.claimText: The University of Auckland's permitted-software assessment guideline says Gen-AI may not be permitted for assessment activities where the assessed skills overlap with functions performed by Gen-AI, and says use of non-permitted software may be considered a breach of academic integrity.
artifacts.normalizedValue: Gen-AI may be disallowed where it overlaps assessed skills; non-permitted software may breach academic integrity

artifacts.sourceTitle: Guidelines on permitted use of software in assessment activities
artifacts.evidenceSnippetOriginal: Generative AI (Gen-AI) may not be permitted for assessment activities where the skills assessed overlap with functions performed by Gen-AI. Use of software which is not permitted may be considered a breach of academic integrity and of the Student Academic Conduct Statute.
artifacts.evidenceSnippetDisplay: The guideline says Gen-AI may be non-permitted where it overlaps assessed skills, and non-permitted software use may breach academic integrity.
artifacts.evidenceLocator: Guidelines: examples of tools and consequences of non-permitted software

artifacts.claimText: The University of Auckland TeachWell two-lane assessment guidance says the University does not endorse third-party AI detection tools, citing unreliability, false positives, and possible student-data training risks.
artifacts.normalizedValue: University does not endorse third-party AI detection tools

citation.sourceRights: Tracker metadata is open licensed. Official source documents, page text, PDFs, and other source materials retain their original rights and terms.

artifacts.sourceTitle: Two-Lane Approach to assessment
artifacts.evidenceSnippetOriginal: The University of Auckland does not endorse the use of third-party AI detection tools, as these may be unreliable, create false positives, and may use student data to train their models.
artifacts.evidenceSnippetDisplay: TeachWell states that the University does not endorse third-party AI detection tools.
artifacts.evidenceLocator: FAQs: What about students who cheat? Can AI detection software help?
artifacts.rightsNote: Tracker metadata is open licensed. Official source documents, page text, PDFs, and other source materials retain their original rights and terms.

citation.sourceRights: Tracker metadata is open licensed. Official source documents, page text, PDFs, and other source materials retain their original rights and terms.

artifacts.notes: Approved as a direct official guidance statement. Claim is scoped to the TeachWell two-lane guidance page.

artifacts.claimText: The University of Auckland's public TeachWell explainer for the AI Usage Standard says users should assess data against the University's data classification before submitting it to an AI tool, and says restricted data should not be used with AI chat services.
artifacts.normalizedValue: AI use must account for University data classification; restricted data not for AI chat services

citation.citationTitle: Learn about the University's AI Usage Standard
citation.sourceRights: Tracker metadata is open licensed. Official source documents, page text, PDFs, and other source materials retain their original rights and terms.

artifacts.sourceTitle: Learn about the University's AI Usage Standard
artifacts.evidenceSnippetOriginal: For example, it requires you to assess any data according to the University's data classification before you submit the data to an AI tool. The University has four data classifications: public, internal, sensitive, restricted. You should only use AI tools that are appropriate for the type of data you're working with.
artifacts.evidenceSnippetDisplay: The public AI Usage Standard explainer says data classification should be assessed before submitting data to AI tools.
artifacts.evidenceLocator: Assess data sensitivity before using it with AI
artifacts.rightsNote: Tracker metadata is open licensed. Official source documents, page text, PDFs, and other source materials retain their original rights and terms.

artifacts.claimText: Auckland Law School's student AI guidelines for taught courses state that using AI to generate, draft, or assist in creating content for graded assignments is prohibited unless the instructor explicitly permits it in writing.
artifacts.normalizedValue: Faculty of Law default prohibition on AI-generated graded assignment content unless explicitly permitted

artifacts.sourceTitle: Student guidelines for using Artificial Intelligence (AI) in taught courses
artifacts.evidenceSnippetOriginal: The use of AI to generate, draft, or assist in creating content for any graded assignment is prohibited, unless your instructor explicitly permits otherwise in writing (in your course outline or on Canvas).
artifacts.evidenceSnippetDisplay: Auckland Law School says AI-generated or AI-assisted content for graded assignments is prohibited unless the instructor explicitly permits it in writing.

artifacts.title: The University of Auckland
artifacts.summary: The staged crawl found official University of Auckland public evidence for a two-lane assessment approach to AI, student responsibility for AI-generated assessment work, academic-integrity treatment of non-permitted Gen-AI use, non-endorsement of third-party AI detection tools, public data-classification guidance for AI use, and a faculty-scoped Auckland Law School graded-assignment rule. The underlying intranet AI Usage Standard was not accessed.
limitations: This report is based on public official sources retrieved on 2026-05-13 UTC for run uapt-university-of-auckland-20260512.
This tracker is not legal advice, not academic integrity advice, and not an official university statement unless a linked source is the university's own official page.
Source evidence is limited to publicly accessible pages. The intranet Generative Artificial Intelligence Usage Standard was not accessed and was marked login-required/inaccessible.
Auckland Law School evidence is faculty-specific and should not be treated as a university-wide rule without additional official evidence.
artifacts.sourceRightsPolicy: Tracker metadata is open licensed. Official source documents, page text, PDFs, and other source materials retain their original rights and terms.
